author: @justinw1765
text: Generally enjoy your style and methods.  Sometimes I wish there would be a little more consistent focus on batteries and supercapitors, but I see value and worth in branching out into different areas as it keeps the mind flexible, the creative juices flowing, and hones a more holistic--systems type approach in a world and education system that tends to push and promote a very linear, overly "left brain" type learning (metaphorically and not literally speaking) and approach. Besides, generating, directing, and harnessing energy is just as important as storing it in the long term.

author: @ronniepirtlejr2606
text: Robert,  would you take a regular (small) satellite dish & point it at a satellite. (Preferably one that's already on your roof, not being used but already correctly aimed) take a four or five foot cable wire made up & connect up to it. You should have between 9 V. - 12 V.  Depending on your signal strength. You should be able to extract around 20 watts of usable power!  That is 24 hours a day/ 7 days a week / 365 days a year! What's a voltage regulator that can be used as an emergency power source or maybe someone can improve on it?

author: @Sheepsville
text:
I absolutely love watching your videos Robert. I am a relative newcomer, having found you during the lockdown. I find you very fascinating and liken you to myself with your inquisitive mind and thought process. I have quite a bit to catch up on but I am also keeping up with your current projects.
However, I did ask you a question about how much power you got out of the oasis, zinc and carbon felt battery thingy and whether it would increase if it was made on a bigger scale. ie. would it power LEDs?
I love electronics and chemistry so you got my attention straight away. Just keep doing what you're doing. It works for me :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: sorry mate - I don't catch every comment these days - there's just too many and nowadays I allocate 3 hours a day to answering comments and posts - so, basically, I apolgise. Anyway, that said, the zinc carbon oasis gives 1.2 volts and the energy is really related to the mass of zinc as long as the zinc surface area is accessible - so yes increasing size will give more energy but if you don't watch out for surface area then the power density will reduce - hope this helps

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text: You make excellent videos  .. don't change a thing. The cold water radiator is a good idea. That's actually how they cool the electronics at the nuclear power plant where I'm sometimes invited. As you probably are aware being able to cool things is at the very heart of a power plant and especially  a nuclear one. From several cool water basins there is a tap to such a cold water radiator and ventilator for rooms where a lot of electronics cabinets are. ( And for this there are backups after backups after backups so they can keep electronics cool no matter what )

author: @didierdelay6623
text: I feel realy sorry for you having to justify the way you do YOUR videos. This channel is yours and those videos are yours. Each and every viewers, including me, we have these contents delivered on our monitor at our will. Nobody forces me to watch, or watch to the end. If a viewer don't feel like their is nothing wrong with shutting down the window and do something else. You do you stuff, the way you want it to. Choosing a certain material against another one is up to you, use a tools more than another one. It's exactly the same about the format of you show. Please, carry on just like you do. For one negative comment, their are 100s, maybe 1000s satisfied and silent people out there.

author: @kezzatries
text: I agree micro stations are the way to go.  I've just built a icf house and every room has its own (second hand)  solar panel,  battery and controller,  plus a small wind power on one of them thanks to you.  The power goes straight to the lights etc and is controlled by remote switching,  cuts down on cable runs and volt drop as everything is 12volt. Failure equals micro failure.  Only thing that I have trouble with is sufficient power for the refrigerator.  Water heater run off fireplace in winter,  solar in summer. 12v ceiling fans direct from PV panels, secondhand again,  in summer.  Thanks mate.  Yore right though you just have to live a little conservatively.

author: @buffplums
text:
Here’s an idea I have that id love to see developed. I’m really interested in drones but the  it thing is the noise of them. It would be interesting to experiment with ways of reducing the noise from the blades. I believe there are things that you can do to the shape of the blades but at the moment I don’t know anything about it. I wonder if some sort of technique that we use in electronics, common mode rejection could be extended to the mechanical environment. Some sort of phase cancellation.... hmmm now her is the germ of an idea...

I once read an article on noise cancellation... it’s probably a heavy and cumbersome solution but what if you had a loudspeaker positioned statically WRT the propeller blades and driven by an audio amplifier fed from a mic picking up the sound of the rotors and phase adjusted (through a sort of PID process) to cancel the noise of the rotors....hmmm

replies.author: @nickhadziannis8451
replies.text:
@Chris cain   I love this.... dont know if you watched a programme called "bugs"  last millennium but they played with noise canceling technology as a story line once.....  caught my attention back then...

Have you seen the drones that have a shield around the proplas and body... the  shape (internal surface geomatry) and  deflects the sound vibraited air into a single vector and creates a kind of vortex ring of sound... Increasing its range but only in one direction.....

Fascinating stuff...   combined with your idea - could = silent running drones....

Scary thought... but also would be fun to develop......

replies.author: @Number_Free
replies.text:
Noise cancellation may work but the timing can be tricky, especially if the noise has to be countered close to the source.

A better approach would be to study the cause of the noise first and then seek to redesign the system so as to make it quieter.

I have been thinking of a hydrogen blimp for example, to reduce the power requirements; that may also be quieter. I want one for geophysics in archaeology.

replies.author: @buffplums
replies.text: Nick Hadziannis yes I remember that series Bugs it was good wasn’t it? It’s certainly an exciting field for investigation I think I will look into this more. Thanks for the feedback!

replies.author: @buffplums
replies.text: Richard Prosser yes I must admit I thought of that idea as well. The only down side is that the device may be affected more by winds but stabilisation could be achieved with a good system of directional thrusters possibly. I was first excited at the idea of drones many years before they came in the market when I saw a video,of a guy who used kites to lift his camera and i started thinking about maybe balloons and then my idea of a lifting platform as I called it came to my head. My idea was a rectangular frame with 4 motors at each corner. But I never did anything with the idea.

author: @Killianwsh
text: I, for one, love trying to guess what final project you're your driving at creating ultimately (prior to your connecting  several stages together) , before you actually make a video talking about it!  To date I haven't guessed correctly yet, but it sure does inspire ones imagination trying to figure the "method to the madness" if you will!  I always learn something from your posts .  :)

author: @buffplums
text: I like the way that you want to share the ideas, as opposed to keeping the ideas for yourself. I used to be of the mindset thinking that everyone was out to steal my ideas but I think when you are open to sharing, that fear seems to disappear and the fun then in doing it returns. The trouble is some are driven by financial gain ... I don’t think there is any enjoyment there but when you collaborate and do something  for the fun of doing it, the experience is far more pleasurable and it’s great sharing thoughts with like minded people.

replies.author: @Sheepsville
replies.text:
Yes, Chris, I can identify with that statement completely. I have kept my 'brilliant' ideas to myself for years, hoping one day, I will make enough money to develop my them, but sadly never have!
It wasn't until I found Roberts' channel, that it dawned on me how much more fun it is to share ideas...
However, The simple fact he has logged his ideas on film with a date attached sort of maintains his original idea; like an open patent (for want of a better way of putting it.) giving him the notoriety he deserves. I feel a lot more comfortable sharing ideas on line for that very reason.
So, if someone were to use an idea he has suggested and developed it for commercial use, potentially making millions, then at least, surely he could he claim to be the founder of the 'invention'?

author: @William_Hada
text: Just keep doing what you hàve been doing. I find your videos fascinating, informative, and inspiring. I must say that the audio and video quality has really improved from the very early ones. The content has always been excellent but the presentation is now top notch.

author: @buffplums
text:
The big thing I’d like to think about is producing an efficient heat pump. Traditional ones with thermal expansion technology as in conventional fridges and also peltier devices but both of which are pretty inefficient. I was inspired a while ago when a young school girl developed her own idea on how to keep water cool in a hot environment, I can’t quite remember exactly what she did but it was passive and involved a process of exchanging heat. Another idea that I found fascinating was the use of wind chimneys in Arabic country’s where winds where directed down a chimney and blew over the stone floor of the house but the process resulted in relatively cool air flowing through the house. 

I’ve always thought how interesting it would be to experiment with different ways of pumping heat away from something and your bellows look like a great starting point.

replies.author: @Number_Free
replies.text: I agree. The graphene ink looks useful, as it has high heat capacity I believe.

author: @Cooliemasteroz
text: I can see the logic in small videos sticking strictly to stages of a project in each one, for the reasons that you gave but also because the following step can go in a number of directions for different projects and this way I see your whole series of videos as building blocks for nearly any project that people can come to to see how to do things for their own unique project. I also like brainstorming though I've never seen it done over a U TUBE channel before and I think it's fantastic. This bellows that you have constructed makes me think of a time back in the early nineties when some friends and I decided to make a radio controlled submarine and through brainstorming all kinds of innovative ideas came up and the main one was to use a bellows to inflate air bags in free flood zones to control it's buoyancy. I'm glad that you like viewers input also as I was a bit worried that I could be hogging the comments section, I don't know how you have the time to do what you do and respond to all the comments. No need to respond unless you want to.

author: @PressToDigitate
text: Your videos have always been awesome.  While it wont help with Summer A/C Cooling requirements, the pieces you're assembling would go quite nicely with the Urine/Aluminum/NaOH Fuel Cell scheme in the Winter, as a useful manner of employing the substantial Heat Energy produced by the reaction.  Suitably configured, the Direct Current could power the Fan and/or Water Pump in the Heat Exchange loop, with the [significant] Hydrogen produced available as well.  At 205 Watt-hours per Aluminum Beverage Can, in principle, a six-pack or so should equal the heating power of a small 1250W room heater for an hour, if the energy released were properly organized.

author: @Patriarchtech
text: I love your approach. Please don't change it. If people don't see the point then it is their problem. What I see here really(sorry to be talking about you in third person) is a guy with tons of knowledge and great communication skills trying to inspire. If people don't understand why incremental steps from a seemingly insignificant experiment could have an impact while being spoon fed, i feel sorry for them.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nicely put mate - cheers

author: @stevetobias4890
text: Thank you Rob, I very much consider you a friend also. This idea exchange makes you so much better than so many others on YouTube for all the reasons you mentioned. I know you are a humble man that doesn't consider yourself better than others but your involving the viewers and listening to them is what sets you apart. You're very much worth supporting in many ways and I appreciate you listening to our ideas as your project evolves. Cheers mate.

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: Another thought is magnets on the piston passing past coils to generate power.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: thank you mate - and I like the magnet idea - I was thinking of storing it in a cap and using it as a pulse to assist driving it - kind of recovering some of the lost energy

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering very good idea about recovering lost energy, we should always try to encorporate this principle in our projects. Even using the expelled air to drive a low friction fan generator on top of magnets on the rod exciting coils to charge super capacitors or batteries which with small 10-20w 12v solar panels will feed back into the system to create a "almost free driven" bellow system. This would finish up being a very, very cool project indeed.

author: @droidclone
text:
A current project i'm working on is an AC as well. I have an A coil from a heat pump I picked up at a local AC place. I have also picked up a windows unit at the local transfer station (where we take trash and unwanted things) and picked up a tossed out window AC unit. I have currently scrapped 1 water pump from a washing machine; untested yet. 

The A coil has 3 different coil paths. Plan is to connect them in series and pass air over them. The condenser coil from the window unit I plan to bury about 3 to 4 feet in the ground. The frost level is about 1.5 feet, so getting it down to 3 feet should net me about 55F year round. 

Thanks for the inspiration from previous videos. I think I have pulled 4 microwaves apart as well for part harvesting.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I like what you are doing there mate - nice stuff

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: Sounds like an interesting concept.  Please do a video on it if it's successful.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
I have a vaguely similar idea, sparked off by Tech Ingredient's evaporative and humidity absorbing AC  concept.  But I want to simplify it and use less energy.  Mine also involves copper coils buried in the ground after the water passes through a solar heated calcium chloride and xylitol concentrator (black fish tank with carbon felt on top).  


  However, my spouse strongly vetoed building a unit connected to the home.  I'm going to try to make a mini, portable version to use with my pickup truck and the "camper top" that I'm building for it (1.5" XPS foam + fiberglass + Titebond 3 primarily).  I figured some Solar panels on top should provide enough juice to run a few fans and a couple pumps. 


  To be used when car camping because it is god awful hot and humid here from late Spring to early Fall, and being of primarily Scottish Highland, Germanic + a little Scandinavian descent--I really don't do well in these kinds of conditions--I'm built for the cold and like the cool and cold.  


   I really prefer real hiking and backpacking, but tend to stick to mid Fall to early Spring for that because of the above reasons.
